Emotion Regulation Worksheet 5 (Emotion Regulation Handouts 8, 8a) (p. 1 of 2)
Check the Facts
Due Date: Name: Week Starting:
It is hard to problem-solve an emotional situation if you donât have your facts straight. You must
know what the problem is before you can solve it. This worksheet helps you figure out whether
it is the event that is causing your emotion, your interpretation of the event, or both. Use your
mindfulness skills of observing and describing. Observe the facts, and then describe the facts you
have observed.
Step
1

Ask: What emotion do I want to change?

2

Ask: What is the prompting event for my emotional reaction?
Describe the prompting event: What happened that led you to have this
emotion? Who did what to whom? What led up to what? What is it about this event that
is a problem for you? Be very specific in your answers. Check the Facts!
Look for extremes and judgments in the way you are describing the prompting event.
Rewrite the facts, if necessary, to be more accurate.
Facts ïš
Step
3

Ask: What are my interpretations (thoughts, beliefs, etc.) about the facts?
What am I assuming? Am I adding my own interpretations to the description of the
prompting event? Check the Facts!
List as many other possible interpretations of the facts as you can. Rewrite the facts, if necessary. Try to check the accuracy of your interpretations. If you
canât check the facts, write out a likely or a useful (i.e., effective) interpretation.
Facts ïš

Ask: Am I assuming a threat? What is the threat? What about this event or
situation is threatening to me? What worrisome consequences or outcomes am I
expecting? Check the Facts!
List as many other possible outcomes as you can, given the facts. Rewrite the facts if needed. Try to check the accuracy of your expectations. If you
canât check out probable outcomes, write out a likely noncatastrophic outcome to
expect. Facts
ïš

Ask: Whatâs the catastrophe, even if the outcome I am worrying about does
occur? Describe in detail the worst outcome I can reasonably expect. Describe ways to cope if the worst does happen. Step

Ask: Does my emotion (or its intensity or duration) fit the facts?
(0 = not at all to 5 = I am certain):
If you are unsure whether your emotion or your emotional intensity fits the facts (for
example, you give a score of 2, 3, or 4), keep checking the facts. Be as creative as you
can be; ask others for their opinions; or do an experiment to see if your predictions or
interpretations are correct.
Describe what you did to check the facts:

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: It is hard to problem-solve an emotional situation if you donât have your facts straight. You must know what the problem is before you can solve it. This worksheet helps you figure out whether it is the event that is causing your emotion, your interpretation of the event, or both. Use your mindfulness skills of observing and describing. Observe the facts, and then describe the facts you

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Open the file without requiring completion.
Fill one field, circle one option, or read one prompt.
Stop, save, or print; partial completion counts.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
